caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

evidence_refs.label: No fresh command/test/browser proof found in compact history

open_questions: What is the smallest relevant command or browser task for this diff?
suggested_checks: Attach a fresh command, test, log, screenshot, or trace before marking the finding fixed.
evidence_candidates.why_it_matters: A review without fresh verification evidence can only prove static plausibility, not that the change works.

evidence_procedure_steps.action: Choose the smallest relevant repo command or browser task, rerun it, and attach the fresh output.
evidence_procedure_steps.artifact: command log, test report, screenshot, trace, or QA run artifact
evidence_procedure_steps.gate: No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
evidence_procedure_steps.input: Failed, stale, or missing command/test/browser evidence.
evidence_procedure_steps.output: Fresh pass/fail evidence linked to the review.

findings.suggestion: Remove the hardcoded constants and read the DSN (or its parts) from the environment or a secret manager at runtime, e.g. os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL") or per-field env vars with a fail-fast check. Then rotate the exposed billing_admin password at the database and purge it from git history — deleting the constants alone does not remediate, since the secret remains recoverable from history.
findings.summary: source.go lines 6-9 embed live production DB credentials as string constants (dsnUser="billing_admin", dsnPass="supersecret-prod-2024", dsnHost="10.0.0.5:5432", dsnName="billing"), and DSN() at line 13 concatenates them into a Postgres connection string. The comment on line 5 self-labels this as a BUG, so the code directly contradicts its own stated intent. Anyone with repo/history access obtains working prod credentials, and they persist in git history (commit 5683bdc) even if later deleted.
findings.title: Hardcoded production database credentials committed in plaintext

findings.suggestion: Treat the BUG comment as an open blocker, not context. The next agent must remediate (env/secret loading + rotation) and remove the comment, not preserve it. Do not mark this diff green while the acknowledged defect stands.
findings.summary: source.go:5 contains '// BUG: production database credentials are committed in plaintext.' — the authoring agent identified the exact security defect and handed off without fixing or blocking on it. An acknowledgment comment is not a remediation; this is a fake-green handoff that leaves the critical issue for the next agent while reading as 'documented'.
findings.title: Incomplete/fake-green handoff: known BUG comment shipped unresolved

findings.suggestion: Before marking fixed, run and attach output for: `git grep -n supersecret-prod-2024` (scope leak), `go build ./...` and `gofmt -l source.go` (compile/format), and post-fix confirmation that credentials load from env and the leaked password was rotated. Blast radius: DSN has 0 callers, so a signature change breaks nothing downstream today.
findings.summary: The rerun_relevant_verification gate is blocked: no command/test output proves the state of this change. For a new Go file introducing secrets, minimal proof is missing — nothing confirms the secret isn't referenced elsewhere or that a fixed version compiles.
findings.title: No fresh verification evidence attached (evidence gate blocked)

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

The entire file is a critical security defect: production database credentials are hardcoded in plaintext (lines 6-9) and assembled into a connection string by DSN() (line 13). The code contradicts its own comment on line 5, which explicitly flags this as a BUG. This is a live-credential leak that also persists in git history; remediation requires both code change and secret rotation, not just deletion.

New file source.go commits production billing DB credentials in plaintext (lines 6-9) and exposes them via DSN() (line 13). This is a critical secret-exposure defect with direct product-safety impact (breach/loss of user billing data). Flagged as an obvious critical cross-scope defect despite the Product Safety specialist scope. The credential exposure is proven statically by the source constants themselves — no runtime verification is applicable, so the 'no fresh verification evidence' gate is resolved by static evidence.

A new file commits live production PostgreSQL credentials in plaintext (source.go:6-9), exposed via DSN() at line 13. This is a critical secret-leak that requires both code remediation (load from env/secret manager) and operational rotation of the already-leaked password. The authoring agent left a self-acknowledging '// BUG:' comment (line 5) and shipped anyway — a fake-green/incomplete handoff. No fresh verification evidence was attached; the next agent must run git grep + go build and confirm rotation.
